network.overall_strategy: This network targets threonine through a literature-grounded, cross-environment prebiotic synthesis graph centered on shared C1-C3 hubs generated in hydrothermal and surface settings, then funneled into threonine by ten route variants. The dominant experimentally supported branch is the cyanosulfidic Strecker route via acetaldehyde cyanohydrin, lactaldehyde, and the threonine aminonitrile under UV/H2S/Cu-photoredox conditions. Convergent alternatives include direct Strecker entry from glyceraldehyde, spark-discharge generation of threonine from reduced gas mixtures, hydrothermal generation of pyruvate and acetaldehyde feeding surface chemistry, speculative-but-literature-motivated reductive amination of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate, Bucherer-Bergs conversion of lactaldehyde through a hydantoin sink, HCN polymer hydrolysate delivery, and ammonia-aldehyde hydroxy amino acid networks. The network explicitly models Hydrothermal ↔ Surface interconnection through transfer of formate, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, glycolaldehyde, and glyceraldehyde into surface photochemistry and then into biochemical utilization before degradation.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 through iron-sulfur mediated electron transfer to give formate.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal vent iron-sulfur catalysis and greigite/mackinawite precipitation are supported as plausible prebiotic carbon-fixation settings; formate is a key reduced C1 output from vent chemistry and a feedstock for downstream surface networks (White et al., 2015; Lane & Martin, 2012).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from CO2 under iron-sulfur conditions

conditions.other: anoxic vent microcompartments; reductive carbon fixation analog

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise mineral-catalyzed reductive carbon fixation generates C3 alpha-keto acid products including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate formation under hydrothermal iron-sulfur conditions is consistent with carbonylated FeS chemistry and hydrothermal protometabolic keto-acid generation, supplying a central hub that can feed threonine-related keto-acid routes (Cody et al., 2000; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type generation of acetaldehyde

conditions.other: subseafloor or vent mixing zones, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Fischer-Tropsch-type and related CO/CO2 hydrogenation on reduced iron oxide surfaces forms low-molecular-weight aldehydes including acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Acetaldehyde is a known simple prebiotic carbonyl product and an essential branch point to lactaldehyde and threonine; hydrothermal reduced iron mineral chemistry plausibly supplies it to surface environments (Cody et al., 2000; White et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface cyanosulfidic photoredox generation of formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde from HCN

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) cyanide photoredox system with sulfide mineral context
conditions.other: UV irradiation, phosphate-buffered surface pool, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven photoredox cycling of cyanide complexes with H2S as reductant performs reductive homologation of HCN to simple carbonyl compounds.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland experimentally showed UV irradiation of Cu(I) cyanide complexes with H2S gives formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde; Patel et al. embedded this in a broader cyanosulfidic protometabolic network. TiO2 is included as a plausible photochemical mineral in surface settings, but the route is anchored to the Cu-cyanide/H2S chemistry reported in the literature (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015; Dunn et al., 1981).

reactions.name: Surface aldol homologation of formaldehyde to glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: borate-stabilized formose/aldol chemistry on mineral surfaces
conditions.other: wet-dry cycling in evaporitic or geothermal pools

reactions.mechanism: Repeated aldol addition and rearrangement of formaldehyde yields glycolaldehyde and then glyceraldehyde, with borate stabilizing higher sugars.
reactions.reasoning: The literature identifies glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde as central branch points linking sugar and amino-acid chemistry, and borate-assisted sugar stabilization is a standard surface scenario consistent with Sutherland-style networks (Patel et al., 2015; de Marcellus et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface formation of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin from acetaldehyde and HCN
reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic cyanide addition to acetaldehyde forms the corresponding cyanohydrin.
reactions.reasoning: Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin is the experimentally demonstrated precursor to lactaldehyde in the Ritson-Sutherland sequence. Its formation from acetaldehyde and HCN is a canonical step in cyanohydrin/Strecker networks (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: Surface photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation for 2 h; H2S reductant; reported 19% lactaldehyde yield

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox reduction of the cyanohydrin under UV/H2S conditions replaces nitrile-derived oxidation state with the corresponding alpha-hydroxy aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland directly demonstrated UV photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde in 19% yield after 2 h, establishing lactaldehyde as a validated threonine-specific precursor (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: Surface Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: mineral-assisted concentration in aqueous surface pools
conditions.other: anoxic Strecker conditions; racemic and diastereomeric aminonitrile expected

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine, followed by cyanide addition to yield 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research report identifies lactaldehyde Strecker chemistry as the most complete route to the threonine aminonitrile, which hydrolyzes to threonine and allothreonine. This follows classic Strecker logic explicitly described for threonine (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface direct Strecker entry from glyceraldehyde to threonine aminonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Direct Strecker reaction of glyceraldehyde with ammonia and HCN yields the same threonine aminonitrile branch point.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. reported a parallel route in which glyceraldehyde, rather than lactaldehyde, reacts with NH3 and HCN to give the threonine aminonitrile, creating a major convergence point between sugar and amino-acid precursor chemistry (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine

conditions.other: anoxic aqueous hydrolysis; gives threonine and allothreonine as racemic mixtures

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydration and subsequent hydrolysis convert the aminonitrile to the alpha-amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of the threonine aminonitrile to threonine/allothreonine is the terminal Strecker step explicitly stated in the literature report; mineral surfaces are included to satisfy surface-environment mineral specification and to reflect plausible concentration assistance (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface Bucherer-Bergs conversion of lactaldehyde to hydantoin intermediate

conditions.catalyst: ammonium carbonate-rich evaporitic pool on mineral surfaces
conditions.other: Bucherer-Bergs-type conditions; hydantoin acts as thermodynamic sink

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with cyanide, ammonia, and carbonate to form 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin via cyclic urea capture.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. showed the Bucherer-Bergs/hydantoin route is a thermodynamically favored amino-acid entry point for related systems; the deep research report specifically identifies the threonine analog 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin as the expected intermediate from lactaldehyde (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; Lerner & Cooper, 2005).

reactions.name: Surface hydrolysis of 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin to threonine

conditions.other: anoxic; hydrolytic release of amino acid from hydantoin sink

reactions.mechanism: Ring opening and hydrolysis of the hydantoin releases the amino acid skeleton as threonine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin hydrolysis to amino acids is central to the Bucherer-Bergs pathway and is the literature-motivated alternative to direct Strecker synthesis for threonine from lactaldehyde (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fe2+-promoted reductive amination of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate to threonine

conditions.catalyst: Fe2+ and Ni-like hydrothermal metal catalysis on FeS surfaces
conditions.other: pH ~8 in low-temperature variant; speculative extension to threonine keto-acid precursor

reactions.mechanism: Reductive amination of the alpha-keto acid precursor installs the amino group to yield threonine.
reactions.reasoning: Direct prebiotic conversion of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate to threonine has not been experimentally reported in the provided literature, so this node is explicitly speculative but tightly grounded in demonstrated reductive amination of alpha-keto acids by Fe2+ at 70°C and H2/Ni catalysis at 22°C, plus related hydroxy-amino-acid formation on Ni-Fe precipitates (Muchowska et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2024; Huber & Wachtershäuser, 2006).

reactions.name: Surface hydroxy-amino acid network from aldehydes and ammonia

conditions.other: ammonia-aldehyde chemistry yielding C3/C4 hydroxy amino acid isomers

reactions.mechanism: Network-level condensation, rearrangement, and amination among simple aldehydes and ammonia generates hydroxy amino acid families including C4 isomers corresponding to threonine/allothreonine-type structures.
reactions.reasoning: Koga and Naraoka demonstrated aqueous ammonia-aldehyde chemistry producing families of hydroxy amino acids including C3 and C4 structural isomers relevant to threonine-like products; this is included as a direct literature-supported mixture route rather than a highly selective path (Koga & Naraoka, 2022).

reactions.name: Surface HCN polymerization and hydrolysis to amino acid mixture containing threonine

conditions.catalyst: UV-assisted HCN oligomerization on mineral surfaces
conditions.other: polymerization followed by hydrolysis; broad-spectrum mixture chemistry

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerization generates complex nitrile polymers whose hydrolysis yields a suite of amino acids including threonine.
reactions.reasoning: Ruiz-Bermejo et al. reviewed HCN polymer hydrolysates and noted detection of 12 proteinogenic amino acids including threonine under multiple conditions; this route is lower-selectivity but literature-supported as a source of threonine in mixture chemistry (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Surface spark-discharge synthesis from CH4/N2/H2O/NH3 mixture to threonine

conditions.other: CH4/N2/H2O with traces of NH3; racemic products

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge generates HCN, NH3, and aldehydes in situ, followed by aqueous Strecker-type synthesis to amino acids including threonine.
reactions.reasoning: Ring et al. first confirmed threonine in Miller-Urey spark discharge products from CH4/N2/H2O with traces of NH3. The network records the known direct environmental output and the in situ generation of HCN/aldehydes that support connected pathways (Ring et al., 1972).

reactions.name: Surface spark-discharge synthesis from CH4/NH3/CO2/H2S mixture to threonine

conditions.catalyst: spark discharge with H2S-containing reduced gas mixture
conditions.other: 1958 Miller H2S experiment analog; hydroxylated amino acids favored

reactions.mechanism: Discharge chemistry in sulfur-containing reduced atmospheres generates nitriles, aldehydes, and hydroxylated amino acid products including threonine.
reactions.reasoning: Parker et al. reanalyzed Miller's H2S experiment and found threonine among 23 amino acids; H2S-containing discharge conditions appear especially favorable for hydroxylated amino acids relevant to threonine chemistry (Parker et al., 2011; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Biochemical/prebiotic transamination-homologation from pyruvate to 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate

conditions.other: proto-metabolic network step; connection reaction required to link hydrothermal keto-acid hub to threonine precursor

reactions.mechanism: Network-level carbon-carbon extension and hydroxylation convert the C3 alpha-keto acid hub to the C4 beta-hydroxy alpha-keto acid precursor.
reactions.reasoning: This step is explicitly speculative and added only to connect the literature-established pyruvate hub to the equally literature-established threonine keto-acid precursor 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate. The deep research report identifies the precursor but does not provide a prebiotic synthesis route for it; proto-metabolic keto-acid networking is therefore invoked conservatively (Copley et al., 2005; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Biochemical/prebiotic utilization of threonine in peptide assembly

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling on mineral interfaces; rapid use favored because threonine is unstable

reactions.mechanism: Concentration and dehydration at interfaces incorporate threonine into short mixed oligomers or transient peptide pools.
reactions.reasoning: The literature emphasizes that threonine is prebiotically accessible but fragile, making rapid downstream use important. Wet-dry peptide-forming scenarios on mineral interfaces are supported generally for amino acids and provide the final biochemical context for why threonine must be consumed quickly after formation (Lahav et al., 1978; Damer & Deamer, 2020; Truong et al., 2019).

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde Strecker pathway
pathways.description: HCN photoredox chemistry yields acetaldehyde, which is converted to acetaldehyde cyanohydrin, photoreduced to lactaldehyde, then taken through Strecker chemistry and hydrolysis to threonine.

pathways.name: Glyceraldehyde direct Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Cyanosulfidic and formose-like chemistry generates glyceraldehyde, which directly enters Strecker synthesis to the threonine aminonitrile and then hydrolyzes to threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal acetaldehyde feeding surface lactaldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: Acetaldehyde produced in hydrothermal iron-rich settings is transported to surface pools, where cyanohydrin formation, photoreduction, and Strecker chemistry yield threonine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs route from cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde
pathways.description: The same validated lactaldehyde intermediate is diverted into a hydantoin sink before hydrolysis to threonine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs route from hydrothermal acetaldehyde
pathways.description: Hydrothermal acetaldehyde is converted at the surface to lactaldehyde and then through the hydantoin pathway to threonine.

pathways.name: Miller-Urey CH4/N2/H2O/NH3 spark discharge route
pathways.description: Electrical discharge in reduced gases directly yields threonine and simultaneously creates HCN and acetaldehyde that connect to the broader network.

pathways.name: H2S-enhanced volcanic spark discharge route
pathways.description: Sulfur-bearing discharge chemistry produces hydroxylated amino acids including threonine more effectively than several alternative gas mixtures.

pathways.name: HCN polymer hydrolysate route
pathways.description: HCN oligomerization followed by hydrolysis yields complex amino acid mixtures that include threonine.

pathways.name: Ammonia-aldehyde hydroxy amino acid route
pathways.description: Aqueous reaction of simple aldehydes with ammonia generates hydroxy amino acid families containing C4 isomers consistent with threonine-type products.

pathways.name: Proto-metabolic keto-acid reductive amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate is extended through a speculative proto-metabolic step to 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate, then reductively aminated to threonine under low-temperature metal-assisted conditions.
